name: Valid Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts to gain initial access, persistence, privilege escalation, or defense evasion.
detection: Monitor for unusual account activity, logon attempts from atypical locations, and use of dormant or service accounts.

name: External Remote Services
description: Adversaries may leverage external-facing remote services such as VPNs, Citrix, and other access mechanisms to gain initial access or persist within a network.
detection: Monitor for unusual remote service connections, especially from unexpected geographic locations or outside business hours.

name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
description: Adversaries may exploit vulnerabilities in internet-facing applications to gain initial access to a system.
detection: Monitor web application logs for anomalous requests, error spikes, and known exploit signatures.

name: Phishing
description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems, often including malicious attachments or links.
detection: Monitor email gateways for suspicious attachments, URLs, and sender reputation anomalies.

name: Spearphishing Attachment
description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with malicious attachments to gain access to victim systems.
detection: Monitor email for suspicious attachments, especially Office documents with macros or executable file types.

name: Spearphishing Link
description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with malicious links to direct victims to credential harvesting or exploit pages.
detection: Monitor email for suspicious URLs, shortened links, and newly registered domains.

name: Trusted Relationship
description: Adversaries may breach organizations by exploiting access granted to trusted third parties such as IT service providers or contractors.
detection: Monitor for unusual activity from third-party accounts and connections from partner networks.

name: Supply Chain Compromise
description: Adversaries may manipulate products or delivery mechanisms prior to receipt by a final consumer to compromise data or systems.
detection: Verify software integrity via hashes and signatures; monitor for unexpected software update behavior.

name: Drive-by Compromise
description: Adversaries may gain access through a user visiting a compromised website during normal browsing.
detection: Monitor browser process behavior, unexpected child processes, and network connections from browsers.

name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.
detection: Monitor process creation for command-line interpreters and log command-line arguments for analysis.

name: PowerShell
description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution, often leveraging its deep integration with Windows.
detection: Enable PowerShell script block logging and module logging; monitor for encoded commands and suspicious cmdlets.

name: Windows Command Shell
description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows command shell (cmd.exe) for execution of commands and batch scripts.
detection: Monitor process creation for cmd.exe and log command-line arguments, especially from unusual parent processes.

name: Unix Shell
description: Adversaries may abuse Unix shell commands and scripts for execution on Linux and macOS systems.
detection: Monitor shell process creation and command-line arguments, especially from unusual parent processes or users.

name: Visual Basic
description: Adversaries may abuse Visual Basic (VB) for execution, commonly through Office macros or VBScript.
detection: Monitor for wscript.exe/cscript.exe execution and Office applications spawning unexpected child processes.

name: Python
description: Adversaries may abuse Python commands and scripts for execution across multiple platforms.
detection: Monitor for python process execution, especially from unexpected directories or parent processes.

name: JavaScript
description: Adversaries may abuse JavaScript for execution via JScript, Node.js, or browser-based exploitation.
detection: Monitor for wscript/cscript executing .js files and unusual Node.js processes.

name: User Execution
description: Adversaries may rely on a user to execute a malicious file or link, often delivered via phishing.
detection: Monitor for suspicious file execution following email delivery or web downloads.

name: Windows Management Instrumentation
description: Adversaries may abuse WMI to execute commands and payloads on local and remote systems.
detection: Monitor for WMI process creation (wmiprvse.exe) and WMI event subscriptions, especially remote WMI activity.

name: Scheduled Task/Job
description: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling to execute programs at system startup or on a scheduled basis for persistence.
detection: Monitor creation of scheduled tasks and cron jobs, especially those executing from unusual paths.

name: Scheduled Task
description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows Task Scheduler to perform task scheduling for execution and persistence.
detection: Monitor schtasks.exe usage and Windows Task Scheduler event logs for new or modified tasks.

name: Cron
description: Adversaries may abuse the cron utility to perform task scheduling for execution and persistence on Linux/macOS.
detection: Monitor crontab modifications and new cron job creation, especially for unusual commands or users.

name: System Services
description: Adversaries may abuse system services or daemons to execute commands or programs.
detection: Monitor for new service installations and service control manager events.

name: Service Execution
description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows service control manager to execute malicious commands or payloads.
detection: Monitor for new service creation events and unusual service binary paths.

name: Account Manipulation
description: Adversaries may manipulate accounts to maintain or elevate access, such as modifying credentials or permissions.
detection: Monitor for changes to account attributes, group memberships, and credential resets outside normal processes.

name: Create Account
description: Adversaries may create new accounts to maintain access to systems they have compromised.
detection: Monitor for account creation events, especially on systems where new accounts are rare.

name: Local Account
description: Adversaries may create a local account to maintain access to a compromised system.
detection: Monitor for local account creation events (Windows Event ID 4720, Linux useradd/adduser logs).

name: Domain Account
description: Adversaries may create domain accounts to maintain access within a Windows domain environment.
detection: Monitor Active Directory for new account creation events and unexpected domain admin accounts.

name: Create or Modify System Process
description: Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads.
detection: Monitor for new or modified system services, daemons, and launch agents.

name: Windows Service
description: Adversaries may create or modify Windows services to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.
detection: Monitor for new service creation and modifications to existing service configurations.

name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
description: Adversaries may configure system settings to automatically execute a program during boot or logon for persistence.
detection: Monitor registry run keys, startup folders, and boot configuration for unauthorized entries.

name: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
description: Adversaries may use registry run keys or the startup folder to execute programs when a user logs on for persistence.
detection: Monitor registry modifications to HKLM/HKCU Run keys and file creation in startup folders.

name: Event Triggered Execution
description: Adversaries may establish persistence by configuring system mechanisms that trigger execution based on specific events.
detection: Monitor for WMI event subscriptions, modified accessibility binaries, and shell profile changes.

name: Modify Authentication Process
description: Adversaries may modify authentication mechanisms to access user credentials or enable otherwise unwarranted access.
detection: Monitor for changes to authentication-related DLLs, PAM modules, and authentication configuration files.

name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities to escalate privileges on a system.
detection: Monitor for unusual process behavior, crashes, and exploitation indicators such as unexpected SYSTEM processes.

name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
description: Adversaries may circumvent mechanisms designed to control elevated privileges to gain higher-level permissions.
detection: Monitor for UAC bypass techniques, unexpected sudo usage, and setuid/setgid binary modifications.

name: Bypass User Account Control
description: Adversaries may bypass UAC mechanisms to elevate process privileges on a Windows system.
detection: Monitor for common UAC bypass techniques such as DLL side-loading in auto-elevated processes.

name: Access Token Manipulation
description: Adversaries may modify access tokens to operate under a different user or system security context.
detection: Monitor for token manipulation API calls and processes running under unexpected security contexts.

name: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification
description: Adversaries may modify domain or tenant-level policies to escalate privileges or evade defenses in enterprise environments.
detection: Monitor for GPO modifications, trust relationship changes, and tenant configuration changes.

name: Indicator Removal
description: Adversaries may delete or modify artifacts generated on a host system to remove evidence of their presence.
detection: Monitor for log clearing events, file deletions in sensitive directories, and timestamp modifications.

name: Clear Windows Event Logs
description: Adversaries may clear Windows Event Logs to hide evidence of an intrusion.
detection: Monitor for Windows Event ID 1102 (Security log cleared) and wevtutil usage.

name: File Deletion
description: Adversaries may delete files left behind by their activities to minimize their footprint.
detection: Monitor for deletion of files in temporary or staging directories, especially after malicious activity.

name: Masquerading
description: Adversaries may manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate to users and security tools.
detection: Monitor for processes whose names do not match their binary paths and files with mismatched extensions.

name: Match Legitimate Name or Location
description: Adversaries may match or approximate names or locations of legitimate files to evade detection.
detection: Compare process binary paths against known legitimate locations for common system executables.

name: Impair Defenses
description: Adversaries may maliciously modify components of a victim environment to hinder or disable defensive mechanisms.
detection: Monitor for security tool process termination, service stops, and firewall rule modifications.

name: Disable or Modify Tools
description: Adversaries may disable security tools to avoid detection of their activities.
detection: Monitor for termination of security processes, modification of security tool configurations, and service stops.

name: Disable or Modify System Firewall
description: Adversaries may disable or modify the system firewall to bypass network-based filtering of malicious traffic.
detection: Monitor for firewall rule changes, firewall service stops, and netsh/iptables commands.

name: Obfuscated Files or Information
description: Adversaries may obfuscate payloads and files to make content difficult to discover or analyze.
detection: Monitor for files with high entropy, encoded command-line arguments, and known obfuscation patterns.

name: Process Injection
description: Adversaries may inject code into processes to evade process-based defenses and possibly escalate privileges.
detection: Monitor for API calls associated with process injection (WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread) and unusual process behavior.

name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
description: Adversaries may deobfuscate or decode files and information to reveal payloads for execution.
detection: Monitor for use of decoding utilities (certutil -decode, base64) and file writes following decode operations.

name: System Binary Proxy Execution
description: Adversaries may bypass process or signature-based defenses by proxying execution through trusted system binaries.
detection: Monitor for system binaries executing with unusual command-line arguments or spawning unexpected child processes.

name: Rundll32
description: Adversaries may abuse rundll32.exe to proxy execution of malicious code via loading DLLs.
detection: Monitor rundll32.exe executions for unusual DLL paths and command-line arguments.

name: Mshta
description: Adversaries may abuse mshta.exe to execute malicious .HTA files and JavaScript or VBScript.
detection: Monitor for mshta.exe execution with unusual arguments or spawning child processes.

name: Subvert Trust Controls
description: Adversaries may undermine security controls that will either warn users or prevent execution of untrusted programs.
detection: Monitor for unsigned or invalidly signed binaries executing in protected environments.

name: Default Accounts
description: Adversaries may use default accounts built into systems or devices that have known default credentials.
detection: Monitor for authentication with known default accounts and enforce password changes on default credentials.

name: Domain Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of domain accounts to gain access within an Active Directory environment.
detection: Monitor for unusual domain account logons, especially from unexpected hosts or at unusual times.

name: Local Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of local accounts to gain access to a system.
detection: Monitor for local account logons from unusual sources, especially administrative local accounts.

name: Cloud Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of cloud accounts to gain access to cloud-hosted resources.
detection: Monitor cloud authentication logs for unusual logon locations, impossible travel, and anomalous API usage.

name: Brute Force
description: Adversaries may use brute force techniques to attempt access to accounts when passwords are unknown or password hashes are obtained.
detection: Monitor for high volumes of failed authentication attempts, account lockouts, and distributed login patterns.

name: Password Guessing
description: Adversaries may guess passwords to attempt access to user accounts, often using common or likely passwords.
detection: Monitor for repeated failed login attempts against individual accounts from single or multiple sources.

name: Password Cracking
description: Adversaries may use password cracking to recover usable credentials from captured password hashes.
detection: Monitor for extraction of password hashes and credential databases from systems.

name: Password Spraying
description: Adversaries may use a single common password against many accounts to avoid account lockout when brute forcing credentials.
detection: Monitor for a single password being used against multiple accounts in a short timeframe.

name: Credential Stuffing
description: Adversaries may use previously compromised credential lists (combo lists) to attempt authentication across services.
detection: Monitor for login attempts from many different username/password pairs in a short window, especially from single IPs.

name: OS Credential Dumping
description: Adversaries may dump credentials from the OS to obtain account login and credential material for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for access to LSASS memory, SAM registry hive, and NTDS.dit file, and known credential dumping tools.

name: LSASS Memory
description: Adversaries may access LSASS process memory to extract credential material stored in memory.
detection: Monitor for processes accessing lsass.exe memory and tools like procdump targeting LSASS.

name: Security Account Manager
description: Adversaries may extract credential material from the Security Account Manager (SAM) database.
detection: Monitor for registry access to SAM hive and volume shadow copy access targeting SAM.

name: NTDS
description: Adversaries may extract credential material from the Active Directory domain database (NTDS.dit).
detection: Monitor for ntdsutil usage, volume shadow copy access to NTDS.dit, and DCSync replication requests.

name: DCSync
description: Adversaries may abuse domain controller replication to extract credential data from Active Directory.
detection: Monitor for directory replication requests (DsGetNCChanges) from non-domain-controller machines.

name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
description: Adversaries may steal or forge Kerberos tickets to enable lateral movement and access to resources.
detection: Monitor for unusual Kerberos ticket requests, RC4 encryption usage, and ticket granting anomalies.

name: Kerberoasting
description: Adversaries may abuse Kerberos by requesting service tickets for service accounts to crack offline.
detection: Monitor for abnormal volumes of TGS-REQ requests, especially for service accounts with SPNs.

name: Golden Ticket
description: Adversaries may forge Kerberos TGTs (Golden Tickets) using the KRBTGT account hash to impersonate any user.
detection: Monitor for TGTs with unusual lifetimes, ticket encryption mismatches, and event ID 4769 anomalies.

name: Credentials from Password Stores
description: Adversaries may search for common password storage locations to obtain user credentials.
detection: Monitor for access to credential storage files, password manager databases, and browser credential stores.

name: Credentials from Web Browsers
description: Adversaries may acquire credentials from web browsers by reading files specific to the target browser.
detection: Monitor for access to browser credential storage files and known browser credential extraction tools.

name: Unsecured Credentials
description: Adversaries may search for unsecured credentials in files, registries, or other storage locations.
detection: Monitor for searches through files for credential-related strings and access to known credential storage locations.

name: Credentials In Files
description: Adversaries may search local file systems for files containing passwords or other credentials.
detection: Monitor for file access patterns targeting known configuration files that may contain credentials.

name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
description: Adversaries may position themselves between two or more networked devices to intercept and manipulate traffic.
detection: Monitor for ARP spoofing, LLMNR/NBT-NS responses from unexpected hosts, and certificate anomalies.

name: Steal Web Session Cookie
description: Adversaries may steal web application session cookies to gain authenticated access as the user.
detection: Monitor for access to browser cookie databases and network traffic containing stolen session tokens.

name: Steal Application Access Token
description: Adversaries may steal application access tokens (OAuth, API keys) to gain access to remote resources.
detection: Monitor for unusual token usage patterns, token requests from unexpected locations, and OAuth consent grants.

name: Account Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of accounts on a system or within an environment.
detection: Monitor for commands that enumerate users and accounts, especially from non-admin users.

name: System Information Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware.
detection: Monitor for execution of system information gathering commands, especially in rapid succession.

name: File and Directory Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories to find information of interest prior to collection.
detection: Monitor for excessive file system enumeration commands, especially targeting sensitive directories.

name: Network Service Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover services running on remote hosts to identify exploitation opportunities.
detection: Monitor for network scanning activity, SYN scans, and connections to many ports on remote hosts.

name: Network Share Discovery
description: Adversaries may look for shared folders and drives on remote systems to identify targets for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for net share enumeration commands and SMB connection attempts to multiple hosts.

name: Network Sniffing
description: Adversaries may sniff network traffic to capture information passed over the network, including credentials.
detection: Monitor for network interfaces being set to promiscuous mode and execution of packet capture tools.

name: System Network Connections Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover network connections to and from the compromised system.
detection: Monitor for execution of netstat, ss, and similar network connection enumeration commands.

name: System Network Configuration Discovery
description: Adversaries may look for details about the network configuration of systems they access.
detection: Monitor for network configuration discovery commands, especially when run in sequence.

name: Remote System Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover other systems on the network that can be targeted for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for ping sweeps, ARP scans, and DNS queries indicative of network host discovery.

name: Process Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get a list of running processes on a system to understand the software environment.
detection: Monitor for process listing commands, especially when combined with other discovery activity.

name: Permission Groups Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover group and permission settings to identify privileged accounts.
detection: Monitor for commands that enumerate group memberships, especially privileged groups.

name: Query Registry
description: Adversaries may interact with the Windows Registry to gather information about the system and software.
detection: Monitor for registry query operations targeting sensitive keys such as security settings and installed software.

name: Software Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover installed software and versions to identify targets for exploitation.
detection: Monitor for software enumeration commands and registry queries for installed applications.

name: Security Software Discovery
description: Adversaries may discover installed security software to inform decisions about how to proceed with an operation.
detection: Monitor for queries targeting security software processes, services, and registry keys.

name: Remote Services
description: Adversaries may use remote services such as RDP, SSH, SMB, or VNC to move laterally within a network.
detection: Monitor for unusual remote service connections, especially between internal workstations.

name: Remote Desktop Protocol
description: Adversaries may use RDP to log into systems remotely for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for RDP sessions from unusual source hosts, especially workstation-to-workstation connections.

name: SMB/Windows Admin Shares
description: Adversaries may use SMB and Windows admin shares to move laterally via file sharing or remote execution.
detection: Monitor for connections to admin shares (C$, ADMIN$, IPC$) from unusual source hosts.

name: Distributed Component Object Model
description: Adversaries may use DCOM for lateral movement to execute code on remote systems.
detection: Monitor for DCOM-related process creation on remote systems and unusual MMC activity.

name: SSH
description: Adversaries may use SSH to log into remote machines for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for SSH connections between internal systems, especially from unexpected source hosts.

name: Windows Remote Management
description: Adversaries may use Windows Remote Management (WinRM) for remote execution and lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for WinRM connections and remote PowerShell sessions between internal systems.

name: Lateral Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries may transfer tools or files between systems within a compromised environment for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for file transfers between internal systems using SMB, SCP, or other protocols.

name: Taint Shared Content
description: Adversaries may taint shared content on network drives or repositories to move laterally when users access them.
detection: Monitor for modifications to files on network shares, especially executable or shortcut files.

name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
description: Adversaries may use alternate authentication material such as hashes, tickets, or tokens to move laterally.
detection: Monitor for NTLM authentication anomalies, Kerberos ticket usage patterns, and pass-the-hash indicators.

name: Pass the Hash
description: Adversaries may authenticate using stolen password hashes without knowing the plaintext password.
detection: Monitor for NTLM logons where the account has no corresponding interactive logon and unusual logon type patterns.

name: Pass the Ticket
description: Adversaries may authenticate using stolen Kerberos tickets without having access to an account password.
detection: Monitor for Kerberos ticket usage from unexpected hosts and mismatched ticket-to-host relationships.

name: Remote Service Session Hijacking
description: Adversaries may hijack a legitimate user's remote service session to move laterally within an environment.
detection: Monitor for session hijacking indicators such as tscon.exe usage and unexpected session switches.

name: Archive Collected Data
description: Adversaries may compress and encrypt collected data for exfiltration to reduce data size and avoid detection.
detection: Monitor for execution of archiving utilities and creation of archive files in staging directories.

name: Data Staged
description: Adversaries may stage collected data in a central location or directory prior to exfiltration.
detection: Monitor for large file accumulations in temporary or unusual directories prior to exfiltration.

name: Data from Local System
description: Adversaries may search local system sources such as file systems and databases for data of interest.
detection: Monitor for unusual file access patterns, especially bulk reads of sensitive file types.

name: Data from Network Shared Drive
description: Adversaries may collect data from network shared drives accessible via the compromised system.
detection: Monitor for bulk file access on network shares, especially from hosts that normally do not access them.

name: Data from Removable Media
description: Adversaries may collect data from removable media connected to a compromised system.
detection: Monitor for USB device connections and bulk file access on removable media.

name: Email Collection
description: Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information from mailboxes.
detection: Monitor for unusual mailbox access, email forwarding rules, and bulk email export operations.

name: Screen Capture
description: Adversaries may capture screenshots of the desktop to collect information on the user's activities.
detection: Monitor for screen capture API calls and image file creation in temporary directories.

name: Input Capture
description: Adversaries may capture user input to obtain credentials or collect information.
detection: Monitor for processes with SetWindowsHookEx calls and unusual input capture behavior.

name: Automated Collection
description: Adversaries may use automated techniques to collect internal data once established within a system or network.
detection: Monitor for scripts or processes that systematically access and collect files from multiple locations.

name: Application Layer Protocol
description: Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols to avoid detection by blending with normal traffic.
detection: Monitor for unusual application layer protocol usage, anomalous request patterns, and beaconing behavior.

name: Web Protocols
description: Adversaries may use HTTP/HTTPS for C2 communication to blend with normal web browsing traffic.
detection: Monitor for beaconing patterns in HTTP/HTTPS traffic, unusual user-agents, and connections to low-reputation domains.

name: DNS
description: Adversaries may use DNS for C2 communication by encoding data in DNS queries and responses.
detection: Monitor for high-volume DNS queries, long subdomain strings, unusual DNS record types, and queries to rare domains.

name: Encrypted Channel
description: Adversaries may use encrypted channels for C2 communication to conceal traffic content from inspection.
detection: Monitor for TLS connections to unusual destinations, self-signed certificates, and anomalous JA3/JA3S hashes.

name: Non-Application Layer Protocol
description: Adversaries may use non-application layer protocols such as ICMP or raw sockets for C2 communication.
detection: Monitor for unusual ICMP traffic volumes, large ICMP payloads, and unexpected raw socket usage.

name: Protocol Tunneling
description: Adversaries may tunnel network communications through another protocol to avoid detection or network filtering.
detection: Monitor for tunneling indicators such as DNS-over-HTTPS, SSH tunnels to external hosts, and encapsulated traffic.

name: Proxy
description: Adversaries may use proxies to direct C2 traffic through an intermediary to avoid direct connections.
detection: Monitor for proxy tool usage, connections through known proxy services, and multi-hop connection patterns.

name: Internal Proxy
description: Adversaries may use an internal proxy to direct C2 traffic between compromised systems within a network.
detection: Monitor for internal hosts relaying traffic and unusual internal-to-internal connections.

name: External Proxy
description: Adversaries may use an external proxy to act as an intermediary for C2 connections.
detection: Monitor for connections to known proxy services, VPS providers, and cloud-hosted redirectors.

name: Ingress Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries may transfer tools or files from an external system into a compromised environment.
detection: Monitor for file downloads via command-line tools (certutil, bitsadmin, wget, curl) and unusual binary drops.

name: Dynamic Resolution
description: Adversaries may dynamically establish connections to C2 infrastructure using dynamic resolution techniques.
detection: Monitor for DNS queries to algorithmically generated domains and rapid domain changes.

name: Domain Generation Algorithms
description: Adversaries may use DGAs to dynamically generate domains for C2, making infrastructure harder to block.
detection: Monitor DNS queries for patterns consistent with DGA: high entropy domain names, rapid queries to non-existent domains.

name: Non-Standard Port
description: Adversaries may use non-standard ports for C2 communication to bypass port-based filtering.
detection: Monitor for services running on unexpected ports and protocol-port mismatches.

name: Data Encoding
description: Adversaries may encode data in C2 communications to make the content less conspicuous.
detection: Monitor for base64 or other encoded data in network traffic, especially in HTTP headers or DNS queries.

name: Data Obfuscation
description: Adversaries may obfuscate C2 traffic to make it more difficult to detect and analyze.
detection: Monitor for unusual traffic patterns, protocol anomalies, and steganographic indicators in network traffic.

name: Remote Access Software
description: Adversaries may use legitimate remote access tools like TeamViewer, AnyDesk, or LogMeIn as C2 channels.
detection: Monitor for installation and execution of remote access tools not approved by the organization.

name: Web Service
description: Adversaries may use legitimate web services such as social media or cloud storage for C2 communication.
detection: Monitor for unusual patterns of access to web services that could indicate C2, such as periodic polling.

name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over the existing command and control channel.
detection: Monitor for large outbound data transfers over known C2 channels and upload spikes.

name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data using a protocol different from the existing C2 channel.
detection: Monitor for unusual outbound protocol usage, especially DNS, FTP, or ICMP with large payloads.

name: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data over an unencrypted non-C2 protocol such as FTP, HTTP, or DNS.
detection: Monitor for outbound FTP transfers, HTTP POST requests with large bodies, and DNS queries with encoded data.

name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data to cloud storage or web services to blend with normal traffic.
detection: Monitor for large uploads to cloud storage services, especially from hosts that do not normally use them.

name: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data to cloud storage services such as Dropbox, Google Drive, or OneDrive.
detection: Monitor for unusual uploads to cloud storage APIs and large data transfers to cloud providers.

name: Scheduled Transfer
description: Adversaries may schedule data exfiltration to occur at specific intervals to blend with normal traffic patterns.
detection: Monitor for periodic outbound transfers that follow a regular timing pattern.

name: Data Transfer Size Limits
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data in small chunks to avoid triggering data loss prevention alerts.
detection: Monitor for sustained small outbound transfers to the same destination over extended periods.

name: Transfer Data to Cloud Account
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data by transferring it to a cloud account they control, such as an attacker-owned AWS S3 bucket.
detection: Monitor for cloud storage transfers to external or unrecognized accounts and cross-account data copies.

name: Automated Exfiltration
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data using automated processing after being gathered during collection.
detection: Monitor for automated scripts or processes that package and transmit data to external destinations.

name: Data Encrypted for Impact
description: Adversaries may encrypt data on target systems to interrupt availability and hold data for ransom.
detection: Monitor for mass file encryption operations, known ransomware file extensions, and ransom note creation.

name: Inhibit System Recovery
description: Adversaries may delete or disable system recovery features to prevent restoration after an attack.
detection: Monitor for vssadmin, wbadmin, and bcdedit commands that delete shadow copies or disable recovery.

name: Service Stop
description: Adversaries may stop or disable services on a system to render those services unavailable to legitimate users.
detection: Monitor for bulk service stop operations, especially targeting security, database, or backup services.

name: Data Destruction
description: Adversaries may destroy data and files on systems to interrupt availability and disrupt operations.
detection: Monitor for bulk file deletion, disk wiping utilities, and MBR/partition table modifications.

name: Defacement
description: Adversaries may modify visual content to deliver messaging, intimidate, or claim credit for an intrusion.
detection: Monitor for unauthorized changes to web content, internal portals, and desktop wallpaper modifications.

name: Endpoint Denial of Service
description: Adversaries may perform endpoint denial of service attacks to degrade or block availability of services.
detection: Monitor for resource exhaustion patterns, abnormal traffic spikes, and application performance degradation.

name: Network Denial of Service
description: Adversaries may perform network denial of service attacks to degrade or block network availability.
detection: Monitor for abnormal network traffic volumes, SYN floods, and amplification attack indicators.

name: Account Access Removal
description: Adversaries may interrupt availability by inhibiting access to accounts used by legitimate users.
detection: Monitor for bulk password changes, account disabling events, and group membership removals.

name: Data Manipulation
description: Adversaries may manipulate data to affect business processes, influence outcomes, or hide activity.
detection: Monitor for unauthorized database modifications, file integrity changes, and data validation failures.

name: File Transfer Protocols
description: Adversaries may use file transfer protocols such as FTP or TFTP for C2 communication.
detection: Monitor for unusual FTP/TFTP connections to external hosts, especially from non-standard source systems.

name: Mail Protocols
description: Adversaries may use email protocols such as SMTP, POP3, or IMAP for C2 communication.
detection: Monitor for unusual email protocol traffic patterns, especially SMTP connections from non-mail servers.

name: Shortcut Modification
description: Adversaries may modify shortcuts to execute their own tools instead of the intended application.
detection: Monitor for changes to .lnk files in startup folders and desktop directories.

name: Hijack Execution Flow
description: Adversaries may hijack the way operating systems run programs to execute their own malicious payloads.
detection: Monitor for DLL loads from unusual paths, path interception opportunities, and modified library search orders.

name: Pre-OS Boot
description: Adversaries may abuse pre-OS boot mechanisms to persist on systems and evade detection below the OS level.
detection: Monitor for firmware modifications, MBR changes, and boot configuration anomalies.

name: Modify Registry
description: Adversaries may modify the Windows Registry to hide configuration, persist, or enable further attacks.
detection: Monitor for registry modifications to sensitive keys used for persistence and security configuration.

name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
description: Adversaries may employ techniques to detect and avoid virtualization and sandbox environments used for analysis.
detection: Monitor for sandbox detection techniques such as checking for VM artifacts, sleep timers, and user interaction checks.

name: Hide Artifacts
description: Adversaries may attempt to hide artifacts associated with their behaviors to evade detection.
detection: Monitor for creation of hidden files and directories, alternate data streams, and hidden windows.

name: BITS Jobs
description: Adversaries may abuse BITS jobs for persistent file transfer and to execute code after OS reboot.
detection: Monitor for unusual BITS job creation with bitsadmin or PowerShell and BITS jobs downloading from external URLs.

name: Exfiltration Over Symmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data over a symmetrically encrypted non-C2 protocol to avoid detection.
detection: Monitor for encrypted connections to unusual external destinations on non-standard ports.

name: Exfiltration Over Other Network Medium
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data over a different network medium than the main C2 channel, such as WiFi or Bluetooth.
detection: Monitor for unexpected wireless or alternate network interface activation and data transfers.

name: Disk Wipe
description: Adversaries may wipe or corrupt disk data and structures to interrupt system availability.
detection: Monitor for disk wiping tools, MBR modifications, and raw disk write operations.

name: System Shutdown/Reboot
description: Adversaries may shutdown or reboot systems to interrupt access or aid in destruction of evidence.
detection: Monitor for unexpected shutdown and reboot commands, especially following suspicious activity.
